title: Collapse with breathlessness

chart.oneLiner: 60F presents after a syncopal episode and now has persistent breathlessness and tachycardia; she has been largely immobile after a recent injury.

opening: I passed out, and now I can't catch my breath and my heart is racing.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 60-year-old woman, breathless and anxious. Cooperative.

HPI: Brief syncope while walking to the bathroom; on coming around she felt very short of breath with a racing heart and some right-sided pleuritic chest discomfort. Largely immobile for 2 weeks with a leg cast after an ankle fracture (only if asked about recent immobility/injury). Mild right calf swelling above the cast (if asked/examined). History of breast cancer on treatment (hypercoagulable; only if asked). NO clear vasovagal prodrome.
PMH: breast cancer (on chemotherapy), recent ankle fracture. MEDS: chemotherapy regimen, acetaminophen. SOCIAL: nonsmoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: tachypneic, mild distress.
- Vitals: BP 104/68, HR 118, RR 26, 37.3°C, SpO2 89% RA.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular; loud P2 if specifically auscultated; JVP mildly elevated if examined.
- Lungs: clear bilaterally.
- Extremities: right calf (above cast) swollen and tender if examined; left normal.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the immobility, cast, calf symptoms, or cancer unless asked about recent immobility/injury, leg symptoms, or cancer history.
- The calf findings, loud P2, and JVP are revealed only if examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Ddimer: Markedly elevated (low specificity given cancer/immobility; pretest probability is high).
labs.CTPE: CT pulmonary angiogram: large bilateral pulmonary emboli with RV enlargement (RV/LV ratio increased) — submassive PE.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia; S1Q3T3 and/or right heart strain (T inversions V1–V3) possible; no acute STEMI.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'ECG Changes in Pulmonary Embolism' library page — download an example tracing that matches: Sinus tachycardia; S1Q3T3 and/or right heart strain (T inversions V1–V3) possible; no acut … and self-host with credit to litfl.com.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: Sinus tachycardia; S1Q3T3 and/or right heart strain (T inversions V1–V3) possible; no acute STEMI.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Often clear — a clear film with marked hypoxemia is itself a clue toward PE.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC normal/near-normal PA chest film.
cxr.imageDescription: Often clear — a clear film with marked hypoxemia is itself a clue toward PE.
searchTerms: chest radiograph Often clear clear film

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this syncope (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Pulmonary embolism (leading & CANT-MISS,9). Cardiac arrhythmia (CANT-MISS,4). Acute coronary syndrome (CANT-MISS,3). Hemorrhage/hypovolemia (CANT-MISS,3). Vasovagal (less likely) (core,2). Connects syncope + dyspnea + hypoxia + immobility/cancer to PE (core,4).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Pulmonary embolism. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Cardiac arrhythmia; Acute coronary syndrome; Hemorrhage/hypovolemia. Also reasonable: Vasovagal (less likely).

coreActions.item: Connects syncope + dyspnea + hypoxia + immobility/cancer to PE

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want? (Consider pretest probability.)
steps.rubric: EKG (core,3). CT pulmonary angiogram (CANT-MISS,6). Troponin and BNP for RV strain (3). CXR (1). ABG (1). Recognizes high pretest probability — proceed to imaging rather than relying on D-dimer (1). PENALTY: relying on a normal D-dimer to exclude PE when pretest probability is high (-3).

penalties.item: relying on a normal D-dimer to exclude PE when pretest probability is high

contraindicatedTests: relying on a normal D-dimer to exclude PE when pretest probability is high

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia; possible S1Q3T3 / right heart strain; no acute STEMI. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (sinus tachy) (6); recognizes right heart strain / S1Q3T3 if present (5); excludes STEMI (2); systematic read (2). PENALTY: calling a STEMI (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes right heart strain / S1Q3T3 if present
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia; S1Q3T3 and/or right heart strain (T inversions V1–V3) possible; no acute STEMI.
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia; S1Q3T3 and/or right heart strain (T inversions V1–V3) possible; no acute STEMI.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Sinus tachycardia; S1Q3T3 and/or right heart strain (T inversions V1–V3) possible; no acute STEMI.

coreActions.item: recognizes right heart strain / S1Q3T3 if present

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear/near-clear film. CREDIT: identifies no consolidation/effusion/pneumothorax (6); notes the clear-film-with-hypoxemia clue toward PE (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: notes the clear-film-with-hypoxemia clue toward PE
criticalFindings: Often clear — a clear film with marked hypoxemia is itself a clue toward PE.
steps.idealAnswer: Often clear — a clear film with marked hypoxemia is itself a clue toward PE.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Often clear — a clear film with marked hypoxemia is itself a clue toward PE.

coreActions.item: notes the clear-film-with-hypoxemia clue toward PE

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the CTPE and RV strain markers, what is your diagnosis and risk category?
steps.rubric: Submassive (intermediate-risk) pulmonary embolism (6). Uses RV enlargement + elevated troponin/BNP, with syncope as a high-risk feature, to stratify (4).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Therapeutic anticoagulation — heparin (CANT-MISS,9). Supplemental oxygen (4). Admit to a monitored setting; close hemodynamic monitoring given submassive PE (5). Consider thrombolysis/escalation if the patient becomes hemodynamically unstable (4). Treat underlying provoking factors; address cancer-associated thrombosis (3). PENALTY: no anticoagulation (-6).

disclosureRules: Do not reveal sensitive history unless directly asked
Do not mention or hint at the diagnosis
Do not volunteer hidden exam or diagnostic findings
Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 60F presents after a syncopal episode and now has persistent breathlessness and tachycardia; she has been largely immobile after a recent injury. The diagnosis is Pulmonary embolism, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: tachycardic, regular; loud P2 if specifically auscultated; JVP mildly elevated if examined.
Extremities: right calf (above cast) swollen and tender if examined; left normal.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Pulmonary embolism while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Pulmonary embolism and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.

State pertinent negatives, not just positives.
